My house was built on a foundation of wood. It works well normally because I put a whole lot of wood under it. 

But termites came and started to eat the wood. Slowly but surely, the wood got thinned out. My house still was fine. Everybody was still living inside comfortably. I ignored the termites since nobody was complaining and didn't add more wood back. The new narrative was a glut of wood anyway, don't worry.

Suddenly, a big storm came. My shaky wooden foundation crumbled. All hell broke loose. But my neighbour next door with cement foundation was fine. 

How I wish I fixed that termite issue when I had the time.

UBS: The probability of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates in July has dropped sharply, but soaring oil prices still make tightening risks difficult to eliminate UBS's latest interest rate strategy report pointed out that U.S.

inflation in June was significantly lower than expected, and the probability of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates in July has dropped significantly. However, the escalating situation in the Middle East and rising oil prices may still push up tightening expectations again.

In June, the overall CPI decreased by 0.42% month-on-month and increased by 3.53% year-on-year; the core CPI decreased by 0.02% month-on-month and increased by 2.59% year-on-year. UBS predicts that core PCE will increase by 0.18% month-on-month and 3.34% year-on-year in June, and determines that May may be the high point of this round of inflation.

After the inflation data was released, the market's pricing for an interest rate hike at the July meeting dropped from a maximum of 11 basis points to less than 3 basis points. UBS expects the Fed to remain on hold in July and to keep interest rates unchanged for the rest of the year as core PCE slows further and employment growth weakens.

However, the market is still pricing in more than one interest rate hike before the end of the year, and expects a cumulative tightening of 42 basis points by April next year. UBS warned that geopolitics is the biggest risk to the above judgment.

After the breakdown of ceasefire negotiations between the United States and Iran, Brent crude oil rose 16% in a week to US$88 per barrel, and crude oil transportation volume in the Strait of Hormuz dropped significantly. If oil prices continue to rise, the Federal Reserve may continue to retain the option of raising interest rates.

Therefore, UBS prefers transactions that can withstand energy price risks rather than directly going long on the front-end U.S. bond duration. It is recommended to continue to hold interest rate swap receiving positions with interests starting in 3 years and a term of 4 years and long 5-year real interest rates.

In terms of the yield curve, UBS has taken profits on steepening trades in 2-year and 10-year U.S. Treasuries and turned it into neutral, believing that long-term yields have risen too fast recently. The bank expects the 2-year and 10-year U.S.

Treasury yields to be 3.95% and 4.35% respectively at the end of the year, and continues to be optimistic about long-term inflation swaps. Foreign investors bought a net $57 billion in long-term U.S. debt in May, the most since November last year, but official demand remains sluggish. Mag 7 is a thing of the past!

Citi: Betting on "Growth Clusters", covering more than half of the S&P market capitalization "Mag 7" was once a core label for measuring the performance of U.S. growth stocks, but as AI investment logic undergoes structural changes, this framework is losing its explanatory power.

Citigroup strategist Scott Chronert's team said in the latest research report that "Mag 7 is dead as a framework for assessing market growth dynamics." The team believes that investors should expand their perspective from a few technology giants to a broader "growth cluster" that covers large technology companies and most related companies that benefit from investment in AI infrastructure.

Behind this change is that the internal trend of "Mag 7" is clearly diverging. Microsoft and Meta are under pressure as the market re-examines the return cycle of huge AI capital expenditures. Apple is favored by funds because of its more restrained data center investment strategy, with growth reaching 23% during the year.

At the same time, the overall performance of the Bloomberg "Mag 7" index has lagged behind the broader market this year, and the traditional logic of grouping is loosening.

In fact, Chronert predicted as early as December last year that the AI trading logic in 2026 will shift from "AI infrastructure providers" to "AI technology adopters", and the current market rotation is gradually verifying this judgment.

Funds are migrating from pure hardware suppliers to application layer companies that can use AI to improve efficiency and improve products. This structural change is more profound than simple sector rotation.

Chip stocks fell into a bear market, JPMorgan Chase called out "summer bargain hunting", but Morgan Stanley continued to bet on cloud giants Amid the deep correction in chip stocks, the two giants on Wall Street have clear differences in their judgments on the market outlook - JPMorgan Chase believes that the time to buy is approaching, while Morgan Stanley insists that hyperscalers are more attractive and has reservations about whether chip stocks can regain market leadership.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) fell 10% in a single week last week, marking its worst weekly performance since April 2025. As a result, it has fallen more than 20% from the historical closing high set a month ago, officially entering a technical bear market. In this context, the J.P.

Morgan strategist team believes that chip stocks "will soon begin to receive buying support" and recommends that investors choose opportunities to rearrange their positions this summer; the Morgan Stanley strategist team predicts that even if chip stocks rebound, it will be difficult to regain their leading position in the second half of this year, and they are more inclined to hold ultra-large cloud computing companies.

Regarding the market trend, Morgan Stanley maintains the S&P 500 index's year-end target of 8,000 points, believing that this target is still possible to achieve.

The team said that the S&P 500's shock consolidation in the past two months was in line with previous expectations, and the market structure was shifting from being driven by a few technology leaders to broader industry participation.

However, Morgan Stanley also warned that if the momentum trading unwinding spreads further or the situation in the Middle East continues to escalate, the S&P 500 may face further adjustment pressure and seek support near 7,000 points. This point is regarded as an important technical support area before the bull market trend restarts.

Morgan Stanley "leads" Wall Street in AI debt trading: earning $2.3 billion in revenue in half a year Morgan Stanley has become Wall Street’s main promoter of building an artificial intelligence financing structure.

The bank continues to devise new debt and equity models to channel tens of billions of dollars into the construction of large-scale data centers. The bank has become a leading financing advisor, leading the largest and most innovative AI infrastructure financing projects since last year, according to industry executives.

These include the exclusive underwriting of a $3.2 billion bond for data center developer TeraWulf, Meta's $27 billion debt financing program in partnership with Blue Owl to build Hyperion data centers, and most recently advising Broadcom on a $35 billion chip financing deal.

The bank's debt and equity capital markets fees surged in the first half to $2.3 billion, up from $1.4 billion in the same period last year, according to LSEG. The performance allowed it to overtake long-time rival Goldman Sachs Group Inc. and move Morgan Stanley into second place behind JPMorgan Chase & Co.

in global capital markets revenue rankings, up from fourth the year before. These deals show that artificial intelligence is reshaping not only the technology sector but also capital markets.

Rather than relying solely on traditional project finance or corporate lending, bankers are increasingly devising new structural models that package long-term computing contracts and the balance sheets of big tech companies into products that can be sold to mainstream investors.

CPU returns to the main stage of AI, Intel’s Q2 financial report faces the most critical battle Intel is at a critical turning point.

As the AI workload structure shifts from training to inference and then to agents, the strategic position of CPUs in data centers has quietly rebounded, and the investment logic of this veteran chip giant is being repriced by the market. Intel will announce its second-quarter earnings report after the U.S. stock market closes on July 23.

This is not an ordinary quarterly performance disclosure - with the stock price rising by more than 160% during the year and the valuation still being at a high level of about 87 times the expected price-earnings ratio, Wall Street requires management to simultaneously deliver on revenue growth, gross profit margin improvement and sustainable growth

guidance, and provide tangible evidence to prove that data center CPU demand and foundry business transformation have entered a quantifiable realization stage. Most analysts are betting that this performance will exceed the company's original guidance, and HSBC raised its target price by 100% to US$200, the highest target among Wall Street analysts.

However, the market's high expectations themselves pose risks.

After Intel announced a better-than-expected outlook for the first quarter, it rose 19% in after-hours trading, and then rose sharply driven by the AI chip boom and expectations of local manufacturing policies; but as of July 17, the stock price had retreated sharply from its June high to around $95, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell more than 20% from its high during the same period, entering a technical bear market.

Whether the financial report can serve as a catalyst for a new round of upside, or whether it will turn into a "good news", will largely depend on Intel's ability to prove its ability to translate AI demand into deliverable capacity and sustainable profits. Boeing's production capacity expansion reaches a critical turning point!

FAA resumes final release authority for new 737 Max and 787 aircraft After a near-catastrophic crash involving a 737 Max aircraft in 2024, regulators found a series of quality issues at Boeing. Subsequently, the U.S.

Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) restricted Boeing's aircraft production and revoked the company's authority to perform final release signatures on new production aircraft.

The FAA said the decision to restore Boeing's relevant authority will officially take effect on July 20, which reflects the company's progress in improving quality management and regaining the trust of regulators. This is crucial for Boeing to expand production, improve its financial position and regain its advantage against rival Airbus.

Currently, the American aircraft manufacturer is working hard to stabilize production of its "cash cow" 737 series aircraft, aiming to maintain monthly production at 47 aircraft, with plans to gradually increase to 63 aircraft in the next few years. In addition, the FAA said regulators will continue to monitor the aircraft manufacturer's quality levels.

Just in: Upgrade to "overweight"! The latest comments from foreign investors are related to Chinese stocks.

Wall Street giants made a big statement. According to the latest news, Wall Street giant Citigroup has adjusted its asset allocation recommendations for emerging markets and raised its rating on China's stock market to "overweight." Citi strategists have given the MSCI China Index a target price of $92 at the end of 2026, with a potential upside of 31%.

At the same time, Citigroup downgraded the Korean market rating from "overweight" to "tactical neutral" on the grounds that the Korean market trading is turbulent and the KOSPI implied volatility has far exceeded similar global markets.

Morgan Stanley also lowered its pessimistic scenario target for South Korea's KOSPI index from 6,500 points to 6,000 points. Citi: Overweight Chinese assets Citi Research wrote in the latest "Emerging Market Equity Strategy Outlook for the Second Half of 2026" that it raised the rating of Chinese stocks from "neutral" to "overweight".

This year's rally led by a few artificial intelligence winners is expected to spread to a wider range of sectors, and the Chinese market is the preferred target for capital rotation. Analysts from the bank pointed out that a true “diffusion market” requires two conditions to be met at the same time: 1.

Evidence of cyclical recovery - macro data improves, and earnings upward revisions spread to a wider range of sectors and regions. 2. The rising momentum led by AI technology has come to a periodic pause - leaving room for catching up with the relative performance of other sectors.

Citigroup believes that, at present, the above two conditions are "partially met." Citi strategists including David Groman wrote in a report that there is room for scope expansion if the macro environment continues to be favorable, including easing geopolitical risks.

Citi pointed out in the report that China is a strong candidate for "spreading the market" - the macro environment of light positioning, falling oil prices and improving global growth is favorable to it, and valuations remain attractive.

Citi China strategist Pierre Lau pointed out that the Hang Seng Index is currently trading at 9.4 times the expected 2026 price-earnings ratio and 1.1 times the price-to-book ratio, both lower than the historical average (10.3 times the price-earnings ratio, 1.2 times the price-to-book ratio).

Citigroup has set a target price of 29,600 points for the Hang Seng Index at the end of 2026 and a target price of 30,500 points in mid-2027; the CSI 300 target prices are 5,600 points and 5,700 points respectively; the MSCI China Index target prices are US$92 (end 2026) and US$97 (mid-2027), respectively, with about 31% upside potential from the current level.

Citigroup maintains the MSCI emerging market year-end target price of 1,870 points, with about 12% upside from the current level, and introduces a mid-2027 target price of 2,050 points (about 20% upside) for the first time.

The target price is based on conservative EPS growth assumptions (about 40%-45%, lower than market consensus expectations) and a slight contraction in valuation multiples. Prior to this, Standard Chartered Bank also maintained an "overweight" rating on Chinese stocks in its report.

The core reason is that there is still a significant valuation discount compared to major global markets. Goldman Sachs also maintains its "overweight" position on A-shares.

Morgan Stanley said that recent global roadshow feedback shows that international investors' interest in Chinese stocks is heating up, and it is expected that there will be a trend of gradual reallocation of funds in the coming months.

UBS Securities China stock strategy analysts said that the profit growth rate of all A-shares this year is expected to increase from 3.9% last year to 11%. Citi also stated in the report that it will not completely withdraw its exposure to AI technology due to short-term fluctuations. The main reasons are as follows: 1.

The free cash flow (FCF) of Asian memory manufacturers is expected to rise significantly from 2026 to 2027, which is in sharp contrast to the FCF of ultra-large-scale cloud manufacturers in the United States approaching zero, indicating that the profit pool of the global technology sector is still expanding. 2.

Citi Korea’s local strategist believes that the memory shortage signal will be further strengthened in 2027, and the trend of memory customization and the growth of AI tokens will promote the continuation of the upward cycle. 3.

The fundamentals of the technology sector in emerging markets remain solid: the EPS growth rate of the IT sector far exceeds that of its global counterparts, profit increases continue, and valuations are attractive compared to similar companies.

Citigroup also said in the report that it downgraded the Korean stock market rating from "overweight" to "tactical neutral" due to the extreme fluctuations in Korean chip stocks in recent weeks. During the session on July 20, South Korea's KOSPI index once fell by more than 5%.

As of the close, the decline reached 4.46%; memory chip giants SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics fell by 4.23% and 4.31% respectively.

Citigroup said that almost all client discussions are currently focusing on the prospect that market performance may "expand" (that is, spread to more sectors) in the second half of 2026, but in the allocation of emerging market countries, the bank is still cautious about fully withdrawing from the technology sector.

At the same time, Morgan Stanley lowered its pessimistic scenario target for South Korea's KOSPI index from 6,500 points to 6,000 points, saying that the index's sharp rise earlier this year made many investors panic and feel overwhelmed.

Morgan Stanley analysts wrote in a report: "Leveraged exchange-traded funds (ETFs) have driven South Korea's KOSPI index and chip stocks to continue to rise, and the market is currently showing trading fatigue." Morgan Stanley maintained the benchmark target of South Korea's KOSPI index at 9,000 points, while lowering the index's 3- to 6-month fluctuation range to 6,000-9,000 points.

In terms of industry allocation, the bank upgraded the communications services sector to an "overweight" rating; it listed the banking sector as its first choice, followed by the information technology, industrial manufacturing and healthcare sectors.

Within the industrial manufacturing sector, defense and military industry is the first choice, followed by shipyards, power generation/nuclear power, and overseas engineering contracting sectors.

Morgan Stanley wrote: "From a medium- to long-term market perspective, the spread of Korean stock market conditions is the general trend; but in the short term, unless reforms bring about revaluation, or the industrial manufacturing sector rises strongly, it will be difficult to significantly reverse the trend of the Korean stock market through market spread alone."

Goldman Sachs is making a reasonable structural argument, but I think they are underestimating the cyclical headwinds and overestimating how much central bank buying can support prices in the face of tighter global liquidity.

The bullish thesis rests on the assumption that central bank demand creates a durable floor for gold. That may be true over very long horizons, but central banks are not marginal price setters in the same way that private investors, ETFs, futures markets, and macro funds are. Gold prices are ultimately determined at the margin, and the marginal buyer remains highly sensitive to real interest rates, the US dollar, and global liquidity conditions.

Goldman's own report acknowledges this contradiction. They estimate central banks bought 81 tonnes in May, one of the strongest months on record, yet gold prices have continued to weaken as markets increasingly price a more hawkish Federal Reserve. That tells you something important. Even exceptionally strong official-sector buying has not been sufficient to offset macro headwinds.

The market is simply shifting its focus. Earlier this year, investors were obsessed with fiscal deficits, currency debasement, and aggressive monetary easing. Today the conversation has changed. Oil prices have risen sharply, inflation expectations are climbing again, and the probability of tighter monetary policy has increased. Rising real yields raise the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset like gold, while a stronger US dollar further tightens global financial conditions. Historically, those have been far more powerful drivers of gold than central bank purchases alone.

I also think the diversification narrative is becoming increasingly crowded. Since Russia's reserves were frozen in 2022, reserve diversification into gold has become one of the most widely accepted structural investment themes. When everyone already knows the story, incremental buying becomes progressively less impactful because much of the adjustment has already taken place. Markets do not price today's purchases; they price tomorrow's changes in demand.

Another point that deserves more attention is positioning. Gold ownership among central banks may continue rising, but private investor positioning remains far more influential over shorter investment horizons. If macro funds, systematic strategies, and ETF investors continue reducing exposure because real yields remain elevated, they can easily overwhelm steady official-sector accumulation.

Finally, Goldman assumes the current rate headwinds are temporary because its economists expect no further Fed hikes. That is a significant assumption rather than an established fact. If energy-driven inflation proves more persistent than expected, or if wage pressures remain elevated, policy rates could stay restrictive for much longer. Under that scenario, real yields remain elevated, the dollar stays firm, and gold could face a prolonged valuation reset despite continued central bank buying.

None of this argues that gold is structurally broken. Central bank accumulation is undoubtedly supportive over the long run and likely limits how deep any selloff becomes. However, investors often confuse having a long-term buyer with having an immediate catalyst. Those are two very different things.

My conclusion remains unchanged. Gold is likely to experience a period of multiple compression as investors rotate toward higher-yielding assets in a world of persistent inflation, elevated real interest rates, and a stronger US dollar. Central banks may provide a floor, but they are unlikely to be powerful enough to reverse the direction of the market if private capital continues flowing elsewhere.

Goldman Sachs is telling clients oil could blow past 110 dollars a barrel as the US and Israeli war on Iran chokes Gulf shipping again, and the bank is pricing that possibility like a weather forecast.

Gulf exports had recovered to more than 80 percent of pre-war levels after Washington and Tehran signed a memorandum of understanding in June, but renewed tanker attacks in the Strait of Hormuz have pushed flows below 50 percent, around 11 million barrels a day. Goldman estimates the market is short 13.4 million barrels a day of Persian Gulf supply. The bank holds its Brent forecast at 80 dollars for the fourth quarter of 2026 and 75 for 2027, while warning prices could overshoot 110 if recovery stalls, or drop into the 60s if production rebounds. A reinstated US blockade of Iranian ports could strip another 1.5 to 2 million barrels a day from Iran's exports.

This crisis exists because the United States and Israel launched an air war on Iran in late February 2026, after which Iran's Revolutionary Guard mined the strait and struck merchant tankers in retaliation. A June memorandum briefly reopened flows before fighting resumed. None of that appears in the market note. What appears instead is a blockade of a sovereign nation's ports rendered as a probability input, stripped of the fact that a blockade is itself an act of war.

Who cashes in on the chokepoint: Goldman's trading desks profit from movement, and a 50 dollar spread between downside and upside scenarios is itself a tradable asset. Gulf monarchies hosting US military infrastructure reroute crude through the Red Sea and collect a scarcity premium. Iranian civilians and stranded tanker crews absorb the actual cost of a war fought to police who sells energy from the Gulf.

My take: Wall Street has turned a war on Iran into a forecasting exercise with no casualties in the model. Goldman's note treats mined shipping lanes and blockaded ports as probability inputs, never as the outcome of a US and Israeli decision to bomb Iran in February. The bank's numbers—an 80 point swing in export flows, a 13.4 million barrel shortfall, a 50 dollar price band—describe a war economy dressed as a supply chain problem. Every dollar Brent climbs is a transfer from ordinary drivers and Iranian civilians to institutions structuring bets on both directions at once.

These aren't just individual stock events — they're the real test of whether AI spending is producing returns or whether cost-of-AI anxiety spreads from chips into the hyperscalers. The Dow's 0.59% decline dragged the financials lower in sympathy, but nothing about today's session changes the thesis. If Alphabet's AI capex commentary echoes TSMC's pattern from last week — good numbers punished on rising costs — tech could get volatile. 整体行情以结构性分化修复为主，很难出现全面普涨，业绩、资金双重加持的赛道反弹弹性更强。早盘受美股上周五大跌、中东地缘利空存在低开惯性，但多重千亿级维稳资金托底，恐慌抛压会快速消化，日内低开探底回升是大概率走势。

五、分层配置思路（优先级清晰）
一、攻守兼备中军（优先布局，波动小、资金加持）
央企低估值龙头：央企千亿增持核心标的，高股息、安全边际高；
银行板块：估值低位、避险属性突出，机构配置共识持续提升。
二、高弹性成长主线（反弹核心主力，中报高增 + 产业催化）
AI 算力 / 高速光通信：Kimi K3 开源引爆算力需求，新易盛、协创数据等中报净利大幅预增，国产超节点、液冷、高速连接器全链条受益；
国产存储芯片：长鑫科技上市，社保、险资大手笔战略配售锁仓，SK 集团预判明年存储供需缺口大幅扩大；
人形机器人：WAIC 落地验证商用拐点，优必选海外汽车产线落地，减速器、伺服、3D 视觉零部件弹性充足；
电网设备 / 电力 AI：南网配网智能体入选 WAIC 镇馆之宝，配网数字化、储能并网长期资本开支稳定，成长 + 防御双属性。
三、地缘避险涨价赛道（对冲原油通胀风险）
钼小金属、油气开采、油运、黄金，美伊冲突持续推升原油价格，具备事件性行情。
四、长线埋伏赛道（回调分批低吸，不追短线）
AI 医疗、合成生物学、太空算力、钠电池 / 钙钛矿新型储能。
短期规避标的
本周大额解禁个股，存在减持抛压；
无订单、无业绩纯题材小票，反弹持续性弱；
高度绑定海外存储、纯外销服务器企业，外围波动仍有扰动。

六、核心风险提示
美伊冲突再度升级，原油暴涨加剧全球加息预期，压制高估值成长股；
美股、韩半导体持续走弱，跨境情绪反复拖累科技板块；
维稳配套政策落地节奏慢于市场预期，反弹力度不及预期；
中报披露期，部分企业业绩不及预期引发个股回调。

Jumping in to "buy the dip" right now before this week's earnings and the upcoming Fed meeting is a high-risk gamble. The macro and fundamental data simply don't support unhedged exposure:

1: The Earnings Stress Test: 
Big tech is entering a critical earnings gauntlet (Alphabet, Tesla, IBM, Intel). Despite TSMC raising its 2026 AI CapeX guidance to $60B–$64B, market patience is wearing thin. Investors are shifting focus from raw AI spending to immediate monetization and free cash flow durability. 

2: Macro & Fed Rate Pressure: 
With 30-year Treasury yields pushing past 5.00% and Brent crude surging back above $90/bbl, inflation risks are resurfacing. The Fed (FOMC July 28–29) maintains a restrictive 3.50%–3.75% target with a hawkish, data-dependent stance, prompting money markets to price in higher-for-longer risks or potential rate hikes by late 2026. 

3: Liquidity & Volatility Squeeze: 
The Tech 100 has already seen a technical breakdown (20/50-day MA death cross), and institutional prime desks report net selling in chipmakers to de-risk ahead of the Fed.

The Strategy:Catching a falling knife when both earnings guidance and central bank policy are in flux is bad risk management. 
🔴 Preserve cash reserves and keep single-stock exposure strictly capped at ≤15%. 
🔴 Stay away from high-decay 3x leveraged vehicles ($SOXL ) and short-dated options—volatility spikes will liquidate you before the thesis plays out.

This isn't a long-term bear call on AI or semiconductors; it's basic capital protection. Wait for post-earnings clarity and the Fed's policy tone before committing fresh capital. Survive first, compound later.

🇮🇷🇺🇸 THE NINTH NIGHT: STRIKES, FIRE, AND A STRAIT ON EDGE

At 10 p.m. ET on July 19, U.S. Central Command completed its ninth consecutive night of strikes inside Iran .

🎯 U.S. Statement on Targets

CENTCOM stated the strikes targeted "Iranian military command centers, air defense and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and communications networks" to degrade Iran's ability to attack commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz .

💥 Reports of Nuclear Site Strike

Iran's Atomic Energy Organization reported that U.S. projectiles struck the Darkhovin nuclear power plant under construction in southwestern Iran at 3:39 a.m. local time on July 19, calling it an "aggressive and barbaric act" .

The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) stated it is investigating the reports, noting that "the facility is in the very early stages of construction and contained no nuclear material when last visited by the IAEA" and that "there is no risk of radiation pollution" .

🔥 Iranian Retaliatory Strikes

· Jordan: Air defenses intercepted three missiles launched from Iran. A fourth fell in a remote area in southern Jordan without causing casualties or material damage. Reports suggested the missiles targeted U.S. aircraft at Aqaba airport .
· Kuwait: A power generation and water desalination plant was struck for the second time in two days, causing fires and affecting electricity supply. Kuwait's air defenses intercepted hostile missiles and drones .
· Bahrain: Air defenses intercepted a number of Iranian aerial attacks. Air-raid sirens were activated nationwide .

The UAE condemned Iran's attacks on Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan, calling them "a blatant violation of the sovereignty of these brotherly nations" .

🛢️ Oil Markets React

Brent crude climbed above $91 a barrel for the first time since June 11, up 3.05% .

📜 Context

The escalation follows the collapse of a June interim ceasefire agreement, which had severely disrupted maritime traffic across the Strait of Hormuz .

Optical communications and storage sectors mostly rose, Lumentum and Credo rose more than 4%, and Marvell Technology rose more than 3%. Livermore's leading index of Chinese concept stocks closed up 1.34%.

European stock markets ended mixed, with Germany's DAX index rising 0.06%. The settlement price of international crude oil futures rose by about 1%. The settlement price of the August contract of WTI crude oil futures closed up 0.9%, and the settlement price of the September contract of Brent crude oil futures closed up 1.27%.

COMEX gold futures fell 0.15% to US$4,012.8 per ounce; COMEX silver futures rose 0.66% to US$56.7 per ounce. [Trump says that Iran will pay multiple times for the death of one US soldier] U.S. President Trump posted on social media platforms on July 20, local time, saying, "Every time Iran kills a U.S. soldier, the price will be paid several times over!

This directive has been conveyed to Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Daniel Cain, and all senior military generals." According to the U.S. military, since the U.S. and Israel launched large-scale air strikes against Iran on February 28, 17 U.S. troops have died in the war, and more than 400 people have been injured.

[The US military says it will launch a new round of strikes against Iran] The U.S. Central Command stated on July 20, local time, that at 4 p.m. Eastern Time that day, the U.S. military began a new round of strikes against Iran in accordance with the president's instructions. U.S.

Central Command said the operation was aimed at further weakening Iran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

[Saudi Arabia-led coalition: Necessary military actions are being taken to ensure shipping safety in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait] The Saudi Arabia-led multinational coalition command issued a statement on July 20, local time, stating that the coalition has begun to take measures to protect alliance ships passing through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, and will take

resolute and forceful responses to any threats to shipping security in accordance with international law and the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea.

[An oil tanker was attacked near the Strait of Hormuz] A recording of the ship's public radio channel near the Strait of Hormuz provided by a seafarer on the evening of the 20th showed that an oil and chemical ship was attacked near the Strait, the fuel tank caught fire, and a large amount of smoke came out of the hull.

The crew was preparing to abandon the ship. The recording showed that the ship was an oil and gas tanker flying the Kuwaiti flag and was attacked in the waters near the eastern exit of the Strait of Hormuz.

The crew of the ship involved reported that two crew members were slightly injured, and a large amount of thick smoke continued to emerge from the damaged areas and fuel tank vents. The crew stated that the fuel tank could not be cooled, and the crew boarded the lifeboat to stand by, preparing to abandon the ship and waiting for nearby rescue forces.

[Trump says Netanyahu will not be arrested in the United States] Trump posted on social media platforms that "(Israeli Prime Minister) Netanyahu will never be arrested in any form or for any reason in the United States." Trump said, "The real ones who should be arrested are the culprits who brought Iran into this unprecedented 'whirlpool of death and

destruction.'" New York Mayor Mamdani said in a recent interview that if Netanyahu goes to New York to attend the United Nations General Assembly, New York City may execute an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court to arrest him. Previously, Netanyahu had gone to New York many times to attend United Nations General Assembly meetings.

[The United States announced an additional 50% tariff on some Canadian products] The White House issued an announcement on July 20, local time, stating that the United States would impose an additional 50% ad valorem tariff on some Canadian products in response to "Canada's discriminatory measures against the United States in the trade of automobiles and auto parts." It is said that the new tariffs will take effect from 00:01 a.m.

Eastern Time on August 19, and will be levied on top of existing relevant tariffs, taxes, and other charges.

[The Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs condemns Ukraine’s repeated attacks on oil tankers operating at the Caspian Sea Pipeline Alliance terminal] Russian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Zakharova issued a statement condemning the attack on the Caspian Sea Pipeline Alliance offshore oil terminal tanker, saying that the attack was carried out by Ukraine.

She said this was the fifth "act of aggression" launched by the Kiev regime against civilian facilities in the alliance. Zakharova said that between July 17 and 20, several oil tankers were attacked by drones while performing loading operations at the offshore terminal of the Caspian Sea Pipeline Alliance.

The attacked ships included oil tankers serving Kazakhstan’s oil industry. The hull was damaged, but no oil leakage or casualties occurred.

Zakharova said that such attacks deliberately cause damage to the economic interests of alliance participants such as Russia, Kazakhstan, the United States and some Western European countries, with the intention of further destabilizing the global oil market. Ukraine has yet to respond.

[New British Prime Minister Burnham appoints multiple cabinet members] The new British Prime Minister Burnham appointed a number of cabinet members on the 20th local time. Burnham appointed former defense secretary John Healey as chancellor.

Healey served as defense secretary in the Starmer government before resigning in June amid a dispute over defense spending. Burnham also appointed Ed Miliband as foreign secretary. Miliband served as leader of the Labor Party from 2010 to 2015 and served as Energy Secretary in the Starmer government.

In addition, Louise Hague was appointed First Secretary of State and Secretary of State for the Duchy of Lancaster. Hague, a former transport secretary who resigned in November 2024, is considered an important supporter of Burnham. Shabana Mahmoud, the Home Secretary in Starmer's government, will continue in her original role.

[Goldman Sachs says hedge funds are selling off U.S. technology stocks at record pace] Goldman Sachs Group Inc's prime brokerage unit noted that hedge funds have exited U.S. technology stocks at a record pace over the past two months. The team headed by Vincent Lin analyzed that such funds have been net sellers of the sector in six of the past eight weeks.

The cumulative reduction in market value reached approximately 10%, the highest level since the data began to be collected more than ten years ago.

“With the semiconductor/storage/AI infrastructure sector continuing to be volatile and heavily sold off, the persistence and scale of selling since early June suggests that technology investors have significantly reduced their positions, and some signs of capitulation are beginning to emerge.” [DoubleLine: Rising U.S.

bond yields will help the Federal Reserve keep interest rates unchanged] DoubleLine is overweight shorter-dated government bonds, arguing that Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh's credibility with investors will help the central bank keep interest rates on hold this year.

Bill Campbell, the company's global sovereign debt and emerging markets portfolio manager, said high U.S. Treasury yields are pushing up borrowing costs and may prompt the Federal Reserve to keep interest rates unchanged if data continues to show inflation is moderating.

The memory chip sector rebounded, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rising by more than 3%, SanDisk rising by more than 6%, and Intel, AMD, SK Hynix, Micron Technology, Seagate Technology, and Western Digital rising by more than 5%.

The optical communications sector continued its gains, with Lumentum and Coherent rising by more than 6%, and Corning rising by more than 3%. China technology leaders collectively rose, Baidu rose more than 4%, Alibaba rose more than 3%, and Tencent Holdings, https://t.co/6YYIAsV2GU Group, NetEase, Meituan, and Xiaomi all rose more than 2%. Spot gold once fell below US$4,000 in early trading and was now quoted at US$4,010.4 per ounce. It fell slightly by 0.15% during the day. Spot silver rose by 1.49% to US$56.71 per ounce. International oil prices fell after opening higher in early trading.

New York crude oil was last quoted at US$81.01/barrel, down 0.94% on the day, and Brent crude oil was last reported at US$87.47/barrel, down 0.72% on the day.

On the news, according to Xinhua News Agency, foreign media quoted a senior Iranian official on the 20th as saying that the Iran-US negotiation mediator has made a proposal to Iran aimed at easing the current situation.

The proposal recommends the implementation of a 10-day ceasefire in order to seek to resume the implementation of the memorandum of understanding reached by Iran and the United States last month.

Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Bagaei said at a press conference that day that Iran has received suggestions from the negotiation mediators between the United States and Iran, and the specific details cannot be disclosed for the time being.

He also said that the Iranian Interior Minister will visit Pakistan that day to discuss matters related to the two countries.

WHY WE'RE FIGHTING THIS WAR
Look, the main objective here is simple and non-negotiable: stop Iran from getting nuclear weapons. Period.Sec. Rubio said it plain — it ain’t America’s job to protect shipping lanes for the whole damn planet forever. But that doesn’t mean we hand the Strait of Hormuz over to the mullahs and walk away. Iran’s regime funds Hezbollah terrorists across multiple continents, including serious networks in Africa, while their crowds chant “from the desert...” calling for the destruction of Jews and Israel. 
To the short-sighted crowd whining about Americans dying — wake up. Your narrow little view ignores the much bigger body count coming if we let these people win.Iran claims they’ve got some “unalienable rights” to control shipping through the Strait. That’s a lie. It’s an international waterway. Freedom of navigation is the rule. They don’t own it and they don’t get to choke it.Sole custody of the Strait would hand Iran devastating power they’ve always craved. They could slap on tolls, block ships, or cut off 20% of the world’s oil anytime they feel like it. That gives them the leverage to dictate terms to China, India, Japan, and South Korea — countries with massive multi-billion-dollar deals with the United States. And what would they do with that power? Supercharge their terror machine. More money and blackmail to expand Hezbollah and proxies on multiple continents, including deeper networks in Africa. More pressure to push their nuclear program forward. More oxygen for the eliminationist chants against Jews that their regime already backs. President Trump’s deals would take a direct hit. The energy stability and open routes those agreements depend on would be at Tehran’s mercy. They’d use the Strait like a weapon to punish our partners and protect their aggression. We’re fighting this war for one core reason: prevent Iran from getting nuclear weapons. The recent framework opens some talks, but success means denying them the power that comes with sole custody of the Strait — the ultimate force multiplier for their terror funding, nuclear drive, and hatred. Sole custody would give them the economic stranglehold to fund more killers worldwide, blackmail their way to the bomb, and keep chanting for Jewish destruction while holding America’s key deals hostage. That’s why we fight. The short-sighted crowd can complain all they want — giving in today just guarantees a much bloodier way tomorrow.

📊 60-Second Market Lesson: EIA Crude Oil Inventories

What is it?
A weekly report from the U.S. Energy Information Administration showing the change in commercial crude oil stockpiles held in the U.S.

Why does it matter?
It's a direct read on U.S. oil supply-demand balance. A build = supply outpacing demand (bearish bias). A draw = demand outpacing supply (bullish bias).

How it moves markets:
🛢️ WTI & Brent crude — most direct impact
💵 USD — indirectly, via energy-linked inflation expectations
📈 Energy equities (XLE, oil majors)
⚠️ Can trigger sharp intraday volatility, especially on a big deviation from consensus

CRUDE OIL - WHAT IS NEXT?

I closed 75-80% of my Brent longs (long futures / sold puts / long UZ and VZ spreads) last week and today. Still keeping a decent amount of oil stocks (half - in smaller names, fairly levered to next 2 years prices, but with little or no debt).

Will probably get yelled at again by fellow oil investors :) - like it happened at the end of April.

Anyway, I was buying dips created by "deal is imminent" announcements by Trump & Co since March 9th, reducing risk in $100-120 area and it worked very well, except for the last time (MOU). I started adding way to early in June and had a larger than expected drawdown. While I ultimately made almost all the money back and overall am up quite a lot since the start of the war, this is not meant to be a victory lap - rather the review of what I got wrong.

The "long oil" trade always had two components:

1) geopolitical (Iran's leverage is proportionate to the tightness of the global oil market & maxed before US midterms / they also made no secret of the plan to have some degree of control over Hormuz). This part worked. I was surprised that the market started to price full recovery right after MOU despite vague language (that actually favoured Iran) and lack of real agreement on pretty much any substantial issue (nuclear / Hormuz). Felt wrong.

In reality it turned out that despite clause 5 of the MOU being much closer to "Iran controls the Strait" than to proper freedom of navigation, US had no intention to cede Hormuz (and the scale of US response was harsher than I anticipated). 

Geopolitical logic favouring long trade (at least from today's 87-91 levels) probably still stands, but I do not feel that I have any edge there. It seems that full normalisation is unlikely for quite a while, but there are tons of scenarios other than the escalation trap. The market could stay in limbo for some time - with partial, but still substantial flows or - eventually - after false starts and episodes of hostilities - come some type of arrangement that gives Iran partial, but not full control (joint management with Oman or other Gulf countries, perhaps in exchange to released funds/sanction relief etc). 

It is fairly clear that despite larger than expected escalation the parties are not targeting energy & export infra aggressively - there were some hits, but they were limited. So while hitting a port or a tanker is probably easier than attacking a US base, Iran has shown certain restraint there - which makes me think that the logic by which Iran will end up with full control because they *could* create a very large problem for oil production/exports does not necessarily work in a straightforward way.

I am not an expert there, so do not want to spend much time on that.

2) fundamental angle. Basically my original idea was that the scale of production loss is so large that the crude prices should stay relatively high at least till year end - and trades with breakeven in mid 80s seemed like a no brainer.

While I do not think now that it was/is necessarily wrong, the fundamental case has weakened.

Lots was said about China imports and flexibility of global refining system (managing to concentrate most of run cuts in petchem). There are a couple more factors that I underestimated:

a) Scale of refining bottleneck. There are 3 components there
- RU 2mbd+ refining losses due to drone attacks
- China. It looks like they are able to manage 2.5mbd refinery run cuts with just 600kbd drop in transport fuel production (which in itself reflects substitution). This is probably not sustainable long term as the petchem part of run cuts in part represents final product destocking, but I honestly do not think they will come aggressively bidding for crude at $90+. They also had around 1.9bln barrels between crude and products inventory (as per GS) before the war so could ease some run cuts by drawing  inventory more heavily

covered it here
https://t.co/HEi3hPznYM

- MEG refiners: it looks like product exports recovery could be lagging crude exports recovery - even desite crazy refining margins (see (b))

Add these 3 and it is easy to see how we ended up with record cracks, very high product prices (approaching demand destruction levels) and meh crude. I certainly underestimated the impact and e.g. closed ICE Gasoil spreads way too early leaving 3/4 of the money on the table.

b) Crude export recovery. It seems to me that the pre-MOU assumptions of the  about crude exports recovery were too conservative. 

"Hormuz shuttle" scheme - when risk-taking NOC (UAE mainly), Sinokor and greek tankers shuttle through Hormuz and S2S to more risk averse owners outside of the Strait really makes a difference here. At the same time there is no similar scheme for products - so product exports and MEG refinery outage recovery would be lagging. And the scheme and high risk environment raise freight costs and hurt premiums  but also benchmark crude prices indirectly (remember that China ultimately cares about landed price).

This again ties to refining bottleneck - if we go back to end of June environment with high refinery outages in China / RU /MEG and relatively large crude outflows from the Gulf we could have moderate crude prices even despite still large production outages, very high products and crazy cracks.

Anyway even though the fundamental bull case is certainly not dead (we do have a large inventory draw - EA estimated it at 650mb across products and crude at the end of June - incl. unobservable; I imagine it should be at least 700-750mb by end of July?;  I am sure that a large part of demand reduction is postponed demand, so it will come back), the trade now leans much stronger on geopolitics.

I do not have an angle there + having a very large long position that makes money on escalation screws with your incentives. To be clear - I do not think there is anything morally wrong with being long or short oil, but I really hate war and would rather sleep better when things are more peaceful ....

CHINA DEMAND

IEA report estimates that China demand dropped 1.6mbd YoY in May. Of thouse gasoline is down only 170kbd (but it was projected to drop 120kbd in the baseline pre-war 2026 demand forecast), diesel @ -330kbd vs projected growth of 45pbd. Jet was down 100kbd vs 46kbd projected growth.

Pre-war they had 2025 and 2026 demand growth of roughly 200kbd, so this means 1.8mbd from baseline.

With crude imports drop around 4mbd (vs average of 2025) and refinerly run cuts of 2.5mbd this implies circa 1.5-2mbd between pause in crude stockpiling (1mbd?) and crude draws (another 0.5mbd).  And another 0.7mbd product draws.

I am assuming above that local production growth YoY roughly matches the pre-war baseline demand growth (in reality it was slightly smaller, but within 100kbd).

Then part of the 1.8mbd demand drop - which is mainly concentrated in petchem - is a mix of pause in petchem product stockpiling and petchem products stock draws; ultimately in steady state at least petchem stock draws should reverse.

Overall, it seems to me that Chinese demand drop is much less dramatic, then is often portrayed - most of the adjustment were indeed stockpiling pause and stock draws, but since they were spread between visible and invisible crude storage, product storage (less observable) and petchem product storage (methodologically does not feed oil stock draws, as those are 1 step down the production chain) each component was either not that big or not visible.

Strait of Hormuz and Global Oil Prices
Market report — July 20, 2026

Executive summary
The Strait of Hormuz is presently the dominant geopolitical risk in the oil market. Renewed U.S.–Iran fighting, attacks on commercial vessels, and sharply reduced tanker traffic have pushed Brent crude back above $90 per barrel, up from below $70 following June’s interim ceasefire. The current price therefore contains both a genuine physical-supply shortage and a substantial security premium. Axios, 

Associated Press
The disruption matters disproportionately because Hormuz normally carries roughly one-fifth of global petroleum supply. Alternative pipelines can bypass only a fraction of this volume, so even partial obstruction rapidly affects freight, insurance, inventories, refinery margins, inflation, and consumer fuel prices worldwide.

Why Hormuz matters
The strait is the only maritime exit from the Persian Gulf. Before the present crisis, it handled:
About 20–23 million barrels per day
Roughly 20% of global petroleum consumption
Approximately 29% of worldwide seaborne oil flows
Major exports from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar and Iran

Asia is especially exposed because most Hormuz cargoes travel to China, India, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia. Europe is affected through global benchmark pricing and competition for Atlantic Basin barrels; North America is less physically dependent but still pays globally influenced crude and fuel prices. U.S. EIA chokepoint analysis
Saudi and Emirati pipelines can divert some production to the Red Sea and Fujairah, but the EIA estimates only around 2.6 million barrels per day of effective unused bypass capacity—far below normal Hormuz traffic. EIA country analysis

What has happened to prices
The market has experienced several distinct phases:
Initial closure shock: After military action and the de facto closure in early March, tanker movements and Gulf production fell sharply. Brent briefly approached $120 per barrel. IEA March report

Persistent physical disruption: Reduced exports caused inventories to fall, regional production to be shut in, and oil to accumulate in locations unable to ship it. On-land stocks dropped by approximately 170 million barrels in April. IEA May report

Ceasefire relief: June’s interim agreement raised expectations of reopening, bringing Brent below $70.

Renewed escalation: The agreement deteriorated, attacks and blockades resumed, and Brent climbed from approximately $86.57 on July 14 to above $90 on July 19–20. Axios bypass analysis, Axios market update

Volatility has also increased dramatically. The EIA calculates that Brent’s average daily price movement during April and May was about $4 per barrel, compared with $1 during the same period in 2025. EIA petroleum-market assessment

How the shock spreads globally
Physical supply: Tankers cannot safely or economically carry normal volumes. Exporting states eventually reduce production when storage fills, turning a transportation disruption into lost global output.

Freight and insurance: War-risk premiums and scarce willing tanker capacity raise the delivered price even for cargoes that successfully transit. Middle East-to-Asia VLCC rates reached their highest level in EIA records dating to 2005. EIA tanker-rate analysis

Refined products: Diesel, jet fuel and petrochemical feedstocks are particularly exposed. Refiners outside the Gulf must bid for replacement crude, while exported Gulf products become harder to obtain.

Inflation and growth: Higher transportation, electricity, fertilizer and manufacturing costs pass through to consumer prices. Import-dependent emerging economies face the additional burden of weaker currencies and larger trade deficits.

Regional differences: Asian buyers bear the greatest direct replacement cost. Europe competes for West African, North Sea and U.S. barrels. American production cushions the United States, but does not isolate its gasoline and diesel markets from global pricing.

Outlook
ScenarioLikely market directionSecure reopening with sustained tanker trafficRapid removal of the security premium; Brent could fall materially, although depleted inventories would slow normalizationLimited escorted passages and intermittent attacksElevated, highly volatile prices around current levelsProlonged severe restrictionRenewed pressure toward the March highs as inventories fall and Gulf production is shut inDamage to export terminals, pipelines or processing facilitiesPotentially more severe and longer-lasting spike than a shipping-only disruption

The decisive indicator is not a political announcement that the strait is “open,” but the restoration of regular, commercially insured tanker traffic. Until that occurs, oil prices are likely to remain unusually sensitive to individual attacks, negotiations and military developments.
